original_title: Using learner corpus methods in L2 acquisition research
replication_title: Investigating the effect of proficiency level on the acquisition order of morphemes for L2 English learners: A replication of a corpus-based SLA study using Interlanguage Annotation
outcome: Article. The annotation covers OCs of (in)definite articles (a(n)/the). Given that the ‘zero article’ was disregarded in previous MOS, it was also disregarded here for comparative purposes. The non-suppliance of an article in an (in)definite OC was tagged as omission. Also, given that both definite and indefinite articles were analysed as a single morpheme in previous MOS, we analysed them similarly, even if we are aware that they are better analysed separately as they do not emerge simultaneously (cf. the discussion in Section 4.4). Selection of one article for the other was tagged as ‘mis-selection’. The forms a/an were understood as spelling variants, so selection of one when the other was expected was not tagged as NTLU. Importantly, determining the OC of the/a(n) is based on information-status distinctions deriving from discourse, i.e., on whether the entity has been previously mentioned in the discourse (i.e., discourse-old, topic) or not (i.e., discourse-new, focus), and therefore whether or not the referent is known to the reader. Articles were annotated independently of the appearance of characters in the picture story, which could give the sense of old information, but technically introduce discoursenew information when retelling the story to a reader. Therefore, ILA considers hearer/reader knowledge (and not speaker knowledge) to determine discourse-old/discourse-new distinctions (cf. Hawkins, 2001).
match_author: Using learner corpus methods in L2 acquisition research
